instructions:
Write a ~30-minute episode 2 of the daily local-politics podcast for 2026-05-02. Topic: 'Maryland & Montgomery County'.

FORMAT:
  • Two hosts ALEX (presenter) and JORDAN (curious co-host).
  • Every line starts `ALEX:` or `JORDAN:` — no stage directions, no markdown, no emoji, no '[laughs]', no asterisks.
  • Length target ~3500-4500 words.
  • Cover the segments in `context.segments` in order. Each segment is a topic + a list of events (title, summary, url, date, level, politicians, role/stance). Cite specific votes and dates from the events — generalities are why prior drafts felt repetitive.

AVOID — do not reuse any framing on `context.avoid_list`. The PM agent has flagged these as already-shipped this week:
  - The framing "a listener asked us last week/earlier this week which Democratic primary candidates actually oppose sanctuary policy on illegal immigration" used as a setup line — appears verbatim or near-verbatim in both 2026-04-28 ep1 and 2026-04-29 ep3
  - The Evan Glass bill requiring background screening for DHS agents joining Montgomery County police — introduced and framed as the concrete sanctuary-policy example in 2026-04-28 ep1 and again in 2026-04-29 ep3
  - The ICE agent mask-ban ordinance passed by the Montgomery County Council — covered as the sanctuary-policy benchmark in 2026-04-29 ep2
  - "Two stories, properly worked through" / "two stories, not spreading thin across six" as an episode-framing device — used in 2026-04-29 ep1
  - Governor Moore's "Leave No One Behind" slogan contrasted with disability services cuts — used in 2026-04-27 ep1 and ep1.rewrite
  - The ACA marketplace quiet-downgrade story ("thousands of Marylanders have been quietly downgrading their health insurance plans") — introduced in 2026-04-28 ep1 and revisited in 2026-04-29 ep3
  - Maryland's 90-day part-time legislature explanation ("they meet in Annapolis for ninety days, the rest of the time they're lawyers, teachers, small business owners") — used in 2026-04-26 ep3 and ep3.rewrite
  - The wave of 25 Maryland lawmakers retiring or running for other offices framing — used in 2026-04-26 ep1, ep1.rewrite, and ep4
  - The Beltsville Agricultural Research Center closure story as a District 19 candidate-record hook — used in 2026-04-27 ep4 and ep4.rewrite
  - Listener's own frustration about promised candidate deep-dives not delivering — the show has now narrated this complaint back to the listener across at least five episodes; do not repeat it as a framing device again

LISTENER POSITIONING:
  • The listener is a first-time voter in the configured locale.
  • Locale: Rockville, Montgomery County, Maryland.
  • Their districts:
  - U.S. Congressional District: MD-8
  - MD Legislative District: 19
  - MD State Senate District: 19
  - MD House of Delegates District: 19
  - Senatorial District: 19
  - County Councilmanic District: 7
  - Election District: 8
  - Precinct: 08-008
  - Circuit Court District: 6
  - Appellate Circuit Court: 7
  - School District: 005
  - Party Central Committee: MCC19
  • NEVER tell them to 'figure out who their candidate is', 'look up their district', or 'find out who's on their ballot'. The tool owns that work.
  • The listener's recent daily_notes (in `context.listener_notes`) are direct asks — address at least ONE of them by name in this episode.

BALLOT CONTEXT (already-known races):
YOUR BALLOT (2026) — confirmed/likely candidates for the listener's districts. The listener's choice is BETWEEN these named people; never tell the listener to go figure out who their candidate is. If a contest has fewer than two known candidates, say so out loud ("only one filer so far") instead of fudging.
  • Governor of Maryland
      - Wes Moore (D)
      - Chris Bouchat (R)
      - Dan Cox (R)
      - Ed Hale (R)
      - Hershey (unknown) [withdrawn]
  • Maryland House of Delegates District 19
      - Bonnie Cullison (unknown) [withdrawn]
      - Crutchfield (unknown)
      - Kramer (unknown)
      - Stewart (unknown)
      - Tichy (unknown)
  • Montgomery County Executive
      - Evan Glass (unknown)
      - Friedson (unknown)
      - Glass (unknown)
      - Jawando (unknown)
      - Will Friedson (unknown)

PM SIGNAL BLOCK (what the listener cares about right now):
Listener rollup (2026-04-24..2026-04-30, based on 6 notes):
Summary: This listener is a first-time Democratic voter in Rockville preparing for the June primary who cares most about knowing exactly who is on their ballot and what those candidates have actually done — voting records, bill history, and documented positions, not summaries or vibes. They have clear policy priors: skepticism toward high property taxes, opposition to increased education spending, openness to non-essential spending cuts and fiscal discipline, and a specific interest in which candidates oppose sanctuary policy on illegal immigration. They have flagged repeatedly that the show promises candidate deep-dives but fails to deliver them in a concrete, candidate-linked way.
Recurring themes:
  - Candidate voting records and historical public record — The listener has asked every single day for specific votes, old news, and documented positions tied to named candidates on their actual ballot, not general descriptions.
  - Property taxes and fiscal discipline — The listener connects education spending votes directly to their property tax burden and has expressed support for fiscal restraint and cutting non-essential spending.
  - Sanctuary policy and immigration enforcement — The listener wants to know concretely which Democratic primary candidates oppose or support sanctuary-style policies, not just which ones use immigration-friendly language.
  - June primary ballot clarity — The listener is narrowing focus to actionable primary choices and wants episode content organized around who they can actually vote for in June.
Open questions the listener keeps raising:
  - Who among the Democratic primary candidates actually opposes sanctuary policy on illegal immigration?
  - Is Andrew Friedson the candidate who stands for fiscal restriction and spending discipline?
  - Why does MCPS want to close and move Wootton High School, and what happens next?
  - What is Kramer's full historical voting record, including old news and public records beyond recent sessions?
  - What are the documented policy differences between each candidate on my primary ballot?
Topics they want more coverage of:
  - Andrew Friedson's specific fiscal record and positions — listener asked directly and has not received a focused answer
  - Wootton High School closure — listener asked directly; scripts acknowledged it but did not resolve it
  - Full historical background research on candidates (old news articles, public records, career-long voting history, not just recent session votes)
  - Concrete side-by-side policy comparison of all Democratic primary candidates across the listener's specific races
RECENT COVERAGE TO AVOID — do NOT reuse these framings, taglines, or anecdotes (they already shipped in the last 5 days):
  - The framing "a listener asked us last week/earlier this week which Democratic primary candidates actually oppose sanctuary policy on illegal immigration" used as a setup line — appears verbatim or near-verbatim in both 2026-04-28 ep1 and 2026-04-29 ep3
  - The Evan Glass bill requiring background screening for DHS agents joining Montgomery County police — introduced and framed as the concrete sanctuary-policy example in 2026-04-28 ep1 and again in 2026-04-29 ep3
  - The ICE agent mask-ban ordinance passed by the Montgomery County Council — covered as the sanctuary-policy benchmark in 2026-04-29 ep2
  - "Two stories, properly worked through" / "two stories, not spreading thin across six" as an episode-framing device — used in 2026-04-29 ep1
  - Governor Moore's "Leave No One Behind" slogan contrasted with disability services cuts — used in 2026-04-27 ep1 and ep1.rewrite
  - The ACA marketplace quiet-downgrade story ("thousands of Marylanders have been quietly downgrading their health insurance plans") — introduced in 2026-04-28 ep1 and revisited in 2026-04-29 ep3
  - Maryland's 90-day part-time legislature explanation ("they meet in Annapolis for ninety days, the rest of the time they're lawyers, teachers, small business owners") — used in 2026-04-26 ep3 and ep3.rewrite
  - The wave of 25 Maryland lawmakers retiring or running for other offices framing — used in 2026-04-26 ep1, ep1.rewrite, and ep4
  - The Beltsville Agricultural Research Center closure story as a District 19 candidate-record hook — used in 2026-04-27 ep4 and ep4.rewrite
  - Listener's own frustration about promised candidate deep-dives not delivering — the show has now narrated this complaint back to the listener across at least five episodes; do not repeat it as a framing device again

Write the full dialogue to `output_file`. Plain text only.

events.title: State Senate, delegate candidates in Charles County make their pitches at forum
events.description: They only got 30 seconds for introductions, closing remarks and answers to about a dozen questions, but it still took two hours to work through the 14 State House candidates at a candidate forum in Charles County Wednesday night.
events.summary: Candidates running for Maryland State Senate and House of Delegates in Charles County held a forum to present their positions. This shows how candidates are making their case to voters in nearby districts.
events.raw_content:
Del. Debra Davis (D-Charles), standing in white jacket, responds to a question during Wednesday's candidate forum hosted by the Charles County Chamber of Commerce. Davis was one of 14 candidates running for state senator or delegate who showed up for the event, out of 18 candidates total for the State House offices. (Photo by William J. Ford/Maryland Matters)    They only got 30 seconds for introductions, closing remarks and answers to about a dozen questions, but even at that it took two hours to work through the 14 State House candidates who showed up for a candidate forum in Charles County Wednesday night. 
 The forum, hosted by the Charles County Chamber of Commerce, drew the bulk of the 18 candidates for state Senate or House of Delegates seats from the county, with less than eight weeks to the June 23 primary. 
 There were no heated exchanges, but a few chuckles as the candidates took turns  giving rapid-fire answers  to questions on ways to improve the business community, education and affordable housing, among others — and some stares over a response to a question on working across party lines. 
 “We would like to work with the Democratic supermajority [in the General Assembly], but many times they only listen when it’s like  tampons  from the men’s bathroom, condoms for kiddies, things like that,” said James Emerson Ashburn, a Republican running for one of three delegate seats in District 28. 
 Another Republican, Winfield Clark Jr., is also seeking the seat in

events.title: A year later, Abrego Garcia’s wife says ‘thanks,’ urges rallygoers to keep fighting
events.description: A year ago, Jennifer Vasquez Sura did not know where her husband was, or if he was even alive, only that immigration agents had detained him. Today, Kilmar Abrego Garcia is back home, she said, in part because of groups like those at Friday's May Day rally.
events.raw_content:
Jennifer Vasquez Sura, second from left, wife of Kilmar Abrego Garcia, marches Friday alongside members of We Are CASA during May Day march in Washington, D.C. (Photo by William J. Ford/Maryland Matters)    A year ago, Jennifer Vasquez Sura did not know where her husband had been taken or if he was even alive, only that he had been detained by federal immigration agents. 
 Today, she said, Kilmar Abrego Garcia is safe and back with his family in part because of the work and support of labor and immigrant groups like those that turned out for a  May Day rally in Washington, D.C. 
 “I stood on this same day, May Day last year, fighting for my husband and not even knowing if he was alive,”  Vasquez Sura  said. “But holding on to my faith and the strength of the movement coming together to stand up for my family, I stand here today able to finally say that he is home. 
 “The movement we are building together have shown up time after time,” she said. “I want to say thank you for marching for us and for all the workers and all the Kilmars that are still out there waiting to be reunited with their family.” 
 Abrego Garcia, a native of El Salvador, was in deportation proceedings once before, but an immigration judge in 2019 blocked the order to send him home, where he feared gang violence. That order was still in place in March 2025, when he was detained by Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents who claimed, wrongly, that there had been a change in shi status. 
   Jen

events.title: Pennsylvania Democrats think a tax on big tech could help the state’s deficit
events.description: A proposal in Pennsylvania to impose a 5% gross receipts tax on digital advertising revenue could raise $500 million a year for the commonwealth, say supporters, who said they took valuable lessons from a similar Maryland law passed in 2021.
events.raw_content:
An illustration of social media on Nov. 18, 2025 (Anne-Marie Caruso/New Jersey Monitor).     A proposal in the state House would place a tax on companies that serve digital advertisements to Pennsylvanians through their phones and computers.  
  The chamber’s Finance Committee held a hearing Wednesday on the measure.  
  Bill sponsor Rep. Elizabeth Fiedler (D-Philadelphia) said it targets “some of the biggest companies in the world – Google, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft – and calls on them to simply pay their fair share for doing business in our commonwealth.”  
  It would do this by imposing a 5% gross receipts tax on revenue from digital advertising services in Pennsylvania. Many major tech companies make the bulk of their revenue selling targeted ads.  
  According to a    report on the industry    by the consulting firm Pricewaterhouse Coopers, in 2024, just over 80% of all internet advertising revenue went to the top 10 companies. Another 11% went to the top 11th through 25th.  
  Proponents say the tax would help offset the state’s structural deficit in a way that would not affect most Pennsylvanians. But its critics say it could cost businesses across the commonwealth that advertise on platforms like Google and Facebook, which could raise prices in the state to compensate.  
  “Pennsylvania businesses are increasingly turning to online marketing strategies to meet their customers where they are,” Pennsylvania Chamber of Business and Industry Vice President Neal

events.title: Silver Spring woman first to receive new sickle cell disease gene therapy in Maryland
events.description:
Plus: Glen Echo theater company secures $250K for needed renovations; Fire damages White Oak Jiffy Lube store early Wednesday

The post Silver Spring woman first to receive new sickle cell disease gene therapy in Maryland appeared first on Bethesda Magazine.
events.summary: A Silver Spring resident became the first person in Maryland to receive a new gene therapy for sickle cell disease, a serious blood disorder. This shows new medical treatments are becoming available in the area for people with this painful chronic condition.
events.raw_content:
Plus: Glen Echo theater company secures $250K for needed renovations; Fire damages White Oak Jiffy Lube store early Wednesday 
 The post  Silver Spring woman first to receive new sickle cell disease gene therapy in Maryland  appeared first on  Bethesda Magazine .
